metrics.narrative: Adonis articulated clear operational pain and business objectives across the 6 May and 13 May calls, but no quantified financial impact (cost savings, ROI, cycle-time reduction, error-rate improvement) was stated by the buyer or modeled by the relevant stakeholder 6 May Adonis described the business problem: replacing a 9-year-old workflow platform with an intelligent end-to-end S2P solution that delivers geopolitical risk insights, supply-scarcity alerts, and proactive contract management to prevent disruption ('before that kind of disruption happens, we want to know we want to have an insight whether we have to make a contract on certain products so the raw material… it will only be impacting our end products, so it will disrupt our supply chain'). He emphasized 'smart procurement' beyond governance—demand forecasting from internal requisition patterns (e.g. quarterly smartphone/notebook needs) and external cost signals (e.g. plastic price spikes from the relevant stakeholder geopolitics) to time vendor contracts optimally and avoid stockouts and price surges. On 13 May he detailed adoption-risk pain: cultural resistance, non-tech-savvy internal users, and SME suppliers at remote locations who could turn procurement into customer service if the platform is too complex. These are real, strategic pains tied to supply-chain resilience, cost control, and user adoption—but none quantified (no baseline spend under management, no cost of current manual processes, no target savings %, no ROI timeline). Guillaume acknowledged the pain and positioned Zycus AI + workflow flexibility + NTUC FairPrice SME reference as the fit, but did not build a value model. The compelling event is the 1 Jan 2027 SAP S/4HANA go-live (deployment synchronized), which is a forcing function but not a quantified business case. Without a quantified metrics case, Zycus cannot justify a price premium in a lowest-cost RFP, and the decision may default to cheapest vendor. Make building a joint ROI model (savings on manual demand analysis, scarcity-avoidance contract savings, adoption-cost reduction via simpler UX) the rank-1 move.

champion.narrative: No champion identified after 56 days and four buyer calls—a critical gap on a competitive RFP with a 'late in engagement vs competitors' handicap. Adonis Sutanto (Governance and Process Optimization, project lead) is the sole Zycus contact across all four calls (6, 6, 12, 13 May) and the only named buyer participant in the Avoma manifest. He is a knowledgeable, engaged technical evaluator and RFP process owner—he articulated pain, requirements, and decision process in depth, asked sharp questions (AI trust, SAP integration, SME UX), and committed to clear next steps (RFP response 20 May, technical clarification week of 27 May, POC June, decision end June)—but he has given zero evidence of advocacy for Zycus over the other three vendors (GEP, Coupa, SAP Ariba). He treated all vendors neutrally in the RFP process ('we don't want to waste too many times… next week I'll be sending the vendors all the RFPs altogether so you guys will have the same same duration, to make it fair for all of you' per 6 May). He pushed back on Zycus's AI positioning with trust/governance concerns and stated an explicit lowest-price expectation ('when we move forward, I'll be expecting your numbers to be the lowest one'), which are evaluator behaviors, not champion advocacy. A champion introduces us to power, shares internal intel, coaches us on how to win, and pushes our solution internally—Adonis has done none of these. He has not introduced the Economic Buyer, CFO, Supply Chain Directors, Chief Digital Officer, or any family-office decision-maker. He has not shared competitive intel (which vendor is ahead, what GEP/Coupa/Ariba are positioning). He has not invited Zycus to any exec briefing or strategic session. His engagement dropped to zero after 21 May (41 days ago)—no response to Guillaume's 12 May offer to reschedule presales follow-up, no outreach to confirm RFP evaluation status or schedule the POC he committed to in June. If Adonis were a champion, he would be pulling us forward; instead, the deal is stalled in his evaluation queue. The second contact, Sigit Rizqi Ramadan (Procurement Excellence, partner role per SF), appeared briefly on the 13 May call as an observer (no speaking role in transcript) and is categorized as a partner contact, not a buyer champion. No other buyer participant across any call, task, or email. The champion gap is structural: in a four-vendor RFP where Zycus entered late vs competitors, the vendor with a champion or coach relationship inside Kawan Lama (someone advocating for them, introducing them to the EB, steering the POC use-cases to their strengths) will win despite product parity. Zycus has no such relationship. Adonis is currently a neutral gatekeeper, not an advocate, and his 41-day silence suggests he may be engaged with another vendor or the deal has stalled across the board. Recommended move: re-engage Adonis within 7 days to confirm RFP status and request an in-person or exec alignment session in Jakarta (Guillaume will be in Jakarta end of month per 6 May) to meet the broader team (EB, Supply Chain Directors, CDO) and build a sponsor relationship—frame it as 'validate the business case and SAP-synchronization plan with the decision-makers before the POC'. If Adonis resists or delays, that is a signal another vendor owns the internal relationship and Zycus is a box-check, not a real contender.

sources: Avoma 6 May discovery call: Adonis explicitly named 'SAP Ariba, Coupa, and GEP… I have talked with them as well… something quite similar to GEP… what do you think will be the major differentiator between GEP and Zycus?'
SF Competitors__c: 'Coupa;GEP Worldwide'
SF Next_Step__c 18 May: 'late in engagement compared with competitors'

Guillaume 6 May differentiation: 'on budget and on time… speed to value… clearest AI roadmap… Zycus superior AI road map and strongest flexibility to adapt to multi geo multi business unit environment' (ST Engineering reference)
No competitive intel or updates since 21 May (41 days)—current competitive standing unknown
competition.narrative: Competition is a four-vendor RFP shortlist confirmed by Adonis on 6 May: Zycus, GEP Worldwide, SAP Ariba, Coupa—all named explicitly in the discovery call when Guillaume asked 'any feedback based on what you have seen' and Adonis replied 'I have talked with them as well… something quite similar to GEP… what do you think will be the major differentiator between GEP and Zycus in terms of solution?'. The Salesforce Competitors__c field lists 'Coupa;GEP Worldwide', matching the call. The competitive picture is live, multi-vendor, and actively being evaluated in parallel as of mid-May RFP response phase, but no fresh intel since 21 May (41 days ago), so current status is unknown. The Next Step log dated 18 May states 'late in engagement compared with competitors', indicating at least one vendor (likely GEP or Ariba based on Adonis's phrasing) engaged with Kawan Lama before Zycus and may have built deeper relationships, shaped requirements, or secured a champion/coach advantage. Threat assessment by vendor: (1) GEP Worldwide: named first by Adonis as the comparison point ('something quite similar to GEP'), suggesting GEP is a known peer competitor in the buyer's mind. GEP is a full S2P suite vendor with procurement-transformation and conglomerate experience (similar positioning to Zycus). Adonis's question implies GEP presented before Zycus and set a benchmark. Guillaume differentiated on 'on budget and on time… we are not dedicating resourcing to customize, we have ability to custom but we are really strong configurating, and in term of speed to value, Zycus is the right option' and 'clearest and more mature AI roadmap'. But no evidence Zycus has a product or relationship advantage over GEP—if GEP is cheaper or has a champion, GEP is the front-runner. Threat level: HIGH (peer product, early engagement, buyer-stated comparison point). (2) SAP Ariba: Adonis listed Ariba alongside GEP and Coupa on 6 May. Ariba is the incumbent SAP ecosystem player, which is relevant because Kawan Lama is migrating SAP ECC to S/4HANA with a 1 Jan 2027 go-live—Ariba can position native SAP integration and synchronized-upgrade story. But Adonis did not emphasize Ariba, and Zycus has SAP-certified S/4HANA adapters (Guillaume positioned ST Engineering as reference for parallel SAP-migration + Zycus implementation). Ariba's classic weakness is complexity and heavy customization (opposite of the 'self-explanatory for non-tech-savvy users' requirement Adonis stressed). If Ariba quotes high or over-engineers, Zycus's configurability + speed-to-value wedge applies. But if Ariba has an SAP-account-team relationship or existing SAP-license leverage, they can win on incumbent advantage despite weaker AI. Threat level: MEDIUM (incumbent ecosystem play, but product-fit gaps on UX simplicity and AI). (3) Coupa: named by Adonis 6 May, listed in SF Competitors__c. Coupa is a modern cloud S2P vendor with strong eProcurement and user-experience reputation. Coupa's historic weakness is AI maturity (Zycus advantage) and conglomerate/multi-BU workflow flexibility (Zycus single-code-base advantage). But Coupa has broad market presence and may quote competitively. If Coupa is the lowest price in the RFP and meets baseline functional requirements, Adonis's stated 'I'll be expecting your numbers to be the lowest one' could favor Coupa despite weaker AI, unless Zycus builds a quantified ROI case to justify premium. Threat level: MEDIUM (strong UX/cloud reputation, possible price advantage, but weaker AI differentiation). (4) Do-nothing / extend current platform: not a real option—Adonis stated 6 May the 9-year-old workflow platform is 'going to be obsolete, we're planning on putting it down to shutting it down like this year', and the 1 Jan 2027 SAP S/4HANA go-live is a forcing function ('we're aiming to have this new procurement platform by 01/01/2027, so it has to be this year to develop it'). Do-nothing is off the table. Threat level: NONE. Competitive summary: GEP is the live peer threat (high), Ariba is the incumbent ecosystem play (medium), Coupa is the modern-cloud/price alternative (medium). Zycus differentiated on AI maturity (Merlin agentic AI, 4+ years Gen AI deployment, first agentic-AI S2P per Guillaume 6 May), single-code-base native S2P integration (vs. GEP/Coupa acquired-module suites), speed-to-value configurability (vs. Ariba customization), and SAP-certified S/4HANA adapters with conglomerate references (ST Engineering, SM Corp, NTUC FairPrice). But differentiation is claimed, not yet proven in POC, and no champion or coach is steering the evaluation to Zycus's strengths. The 'late in engagement' note + 41-day silence raises the risk a competitor (likely GEP or Ariba) is actively engaging while Zycus waits, and may have built the EB/sponsor relationship Zycus lacks. The wedge to win: prove governed, trustworthy AI with visible human-in-the-loop controls in the POC (address Adonis's trust concern), demonstrate self-explanatory SME-supplier UX (address adoption pain), and build a quantified ROI model showing speed-to-value + intelligent-procurement savings justify any price premium over GEP/Coupa (counter the lowest-price expectation). If Zycus cannot prove these three in the POC and cannot map the Economic Buyer to build exec sponsorship, GEP or Ariba will win on relationship or price despite weaker product.

SF Next_Step__c 18 May: 'good engagement, potential 1st reference in Indonesia'—implies pain is strategic (reference-case opportunity) not just tactical
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is clearly identified and articulated by Adonis across the 6 May and 13 May calls, covering operational inefficiency, supply-chain risk, adoption barriers, and AI-governance anxiety—confirmed pain owned by corporate procurement (Adonis project lead) and ultimately the family business ownership (per Adonis 6 May). The pain has four dimensions: (1) Operational: the current 9-year-old workflow platform is obsolete, being shut down this year, forcing a replacement by 1 Jan 2027 to align with SAP S/4HANA migration. Manual demand planning and market-intelligence gathering (Adonis: 'we're still doing manually, going to different portals') wastes time and misses optimal contract-timing windows, leading to stockouts or price surges. Decentralized vendor databases (PMS, iCPS, future eProcurement) create inconsistent data, blocking prequalification and tender workflows. Delayed goods-receipt logging (users must return to office and desktop to do GR, sometimes forget) disrupts procurement close-out. (2) Supply-chain risk: lack of intelligent geopolitical-risk and supply-scarcity signals means procurement reacts to disruptions instead of contracting proactively ('before the product or before the materials are like being hard to source, now we have to get a contract with the supplier… before the goods getting really really hard to… do risk parts at the market'). Example: Middle East geopolitics spiked plastic prices; Kawan Lama had no advance warning to lock contracts. Internal demand forecasting from requisition patterns (quarterly smartphone/notebook needs) is manual and reactive, causing urgent last-minute buys when items unavailable. (3) Adoption/change-management: cultural resistance to change from previous system/process ('adoption to change and to transform… could be quite difficult because of our own culture'), non-tech-savvy internal users, and SME suppliers at remote locations who may turn procurement into customer service if platform too complex ('if we're having too complex, too sophisticated application… the procurement team will be becoming a customer service because we'll have to explain them how to use it'). User-interface simplicity for low-tech users is a veto criterion. (4) AI trust/governance: family-owned business requires high trust and control over AI agents that handle procurement money; Adonis expressed concern about AI reliability, rule-violations, and 'cases in the real world where AI agentic AI sometimes ruining something' (6 May). Without visible constraints and human-in-the-loop governance, AI positioning is a risk, not a wedge. Pain owners: Adonis (project lead, owns RFP process and requirements), corporate procurement team (operational pain), Supply Chain Directors/CFO/Chief Digital Officer (budget and strategic pain, though not yet engaged by Zycus), and ultimately family business ownership (trust/governance pain per Adonis). Pain is real, urgent (1 Jan 2027 forcing function), and multi-dimensional—but not yet quantified (no cost of current pain, no ROI model). Zycus positioned AI + workflow-flexibility + SAP-certified-adapters + NTUC-FairPrice-SME-reference as the fit, but has not translated fit into a financial business case Adonis can use to justify a price premium in a lowest-cost RFP. Recommended move: co-build a quantified pain-to-value model (cost of manual demand-intel + scarcity-driven price-spike losses + adoption-friction support costs vs. Zycus AI savings + faster time-to-value) with Adonis within 14 days, anchored to the four pain dimensions, to shift the RFP conversation from price to ROI.

paper_process.narrative: Paper process and contracting mechanics are partially understood from the 13 May technical call but not yet formalized or scoped with legal/procurement, and no MSA, SOW, or security-review timeline has been discussed. Adonis described on 13 May a dual contractual approach: (1) PPH (legally-based contractual agreement): offline price-only contracts with vendors (no quantity or purchase commitment) for strategic-sourcing item catalogs, enabling fast transactions without a PR—'legal document'. (2) OA (Outline Agreement in SAP): SAP-integrated version of the contract for transactional execution; the eProcurement system must integrate with SAP to create the OA ('it needs to be integrated from this eProcurement, so it needs to tell the SAP to create the OA'). Both will continue in future state. This suggests contract-creation workflow and SAP-contract-integration scope must be part of the SOW, but no legal review, MSA/Order-Form negotiation, or InfoSec questionnaire has been initiated. The RFP process as described by Adonis (6 May, 12 May) goes: RFP response → technical clarification → POC → vendor selection approval → awarding, then 'deployment start June or July'. No mention of contract negotiation, legal review, security review, or procurement-channel steps between award and deployment—implying either (a) those steps are collapsed into a fast post-award phase, or (b) they are unplanned/underestimated and will cause the real slip. Given this is a $250k spend synchronized to a 1 Jan 2027 ERP migration, procurement and legal sign-off should be multi-week gates. The forcing function (Current_Contract_Expiration__c if the 9-year-old workflow platform has a contract end-date, or the 1 Jan 2027 SAP go-live) is real, but the paper-process timeline to get there is opaque. No InfoSec/compliance review mentioned despite this being a centralized procurement platform handling supplier data across subsidiaries. The 41-day silence since 21 May could include legal/procurement holds we are not aware of. Recommended move: after re-engaging Adonis on RFP status, explicitly map the post-award contract timeline (legal review, MSA/SOW, InfoSec, SAP-integration deep-dive, contract signature) and work backward from 1 Jan 2027 go-live to set a realistic close date and expose any hidden gates, within 14 days.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is a structured four-vendor RFP with defined milestones, but execution is now stalled 41 days past last buyer contact, and the timeline Adonis committed to in mid-May (vendor decision end June, award June) has not materialized as of 1 July. Adonis detailed the process on 6 May: (1) Prequalification: vendors register, sign integrity-pact NDA, complete questionnaire before receiving RFP. (2) RFP issuance: planned week of 13 May to four vendors (Zycus, GEP, Coupa, SAP Ariba). (3) RFP response: due 20 May 2026 (per 12 May call). (4) Technical clarification session: week of 27 May, one-on-one with each vendor to discuss proposals and align on requirements; proposal revisions follow. (5) POC/demo: one to two weeks in June, hands-on prototype or demo with specific use cases. (6) Evaluation and approval: internal scoring, vendor comparison, approval process to select winner by end June. (7) Awarding: June 2026. (8) Deployment: start June or July, complete by end 2026, go-live 1 Jan 2027 synchronized with SAP S/4HANA migration. The call sequence itself shows the process in action: 6 May intro call (discovery, scoping, NDA/prequalification explained), 12 May clarification scheduling attempt (presales team no-show, rescheduled by email), 13 May deep technical clarification (VMS, taxonomy, PPH/OA, mobile, market intel), then silence. The 18 May Next Step log says 'RFP received, QIT submitted, working on RFP response', indicating Zycus responded on or before 20 May. But no technical clarification call logged week of 27 May, no POC scheduled in June, no vendor-selection milestone hit end June. As of 1 July the process has stalled in the post-RFP evaluation phase. Approval chain: Adonis mentioned 6 May 'evaluation and approval process' (passive) and 'company owned by family' (suggesting family-office or ownership sign-off above the procurement team), but he never named the approvers—CFO, Supply Chain Directors, Chief Digital Officer, or family principals are invisible. The opaque approval chain + 41-day silence + competitor 'late in engagement' note = high risk another vendor is navigating the approval process we cannot see. The realistic decision timeline is now July-Aug (2-month slip from 'end June'), pushing contract + deployment into Q4 2026 and close into Q1 2027, not 1 Dec 2026. Recommended move: re-engage Adonis immediately (within 7 days) to confirm RFP evaluation status, request the technical clarification and POC slots, and surface any blockers or competitive moves in the silent 41-day window.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are well-documented from the RFP discovery (6 May) and technical clarification (13 May) calls, covering functional, technical, adoption, and AI-governance dimensions—but not yet validated in a POC or weighted/scored by the buyer, so they remain stated criteria vs. proven fit. On 6 May Adonis outlined the core requirements: (1) End-to-end S2P coverage (sourcing, contracts, eProcurement, supplier mgmt, spend analytics) with strong workflow flexibility to accommodate multiple subsidiaries, industries, and business units ('two different industry, own manufacturing, own DC, each have their own interest, specific priorities, this is process that we have to cater'). (2) Intelligent insights: geopolitical risk alerts, supply-scarcity signals, demand forecasting from internal requisition patterns, and external market price signals to time vendor contracts optimally—'smart procurement', not just governance. (3) Native SAP ECC and S/4HANA integration with certified adapters, synchronized go-live 1 Jan 2027 ('we want this to be in line, same timeline'). (4) User-friendly, mobile-responsive interface for non-tech-savvy internal users and SME suppliers at remote locations, minimizing support burden ('if too complex, procurement team will be becoming a customer service'). (5) Agentic AI capabilities with visible governance, constraints, audit trails, and human-in-the-loop controls to satisfy a risk-averse family-owned business ('how to set a constraint or guide rails… when we're talking about money… trust is something that need to be convinced'). (6) Phased AI adoption starting with conversational/decisional AI quick wins before full agentic autonomy (Guillaume's recommendation, Adonis accepted). On 13 May technical session added: (7) Centralized vendor master data management (VMS integration via Kafka/Red Panda) enforcing prequalification workflow before sourcing. (8) Hierarchical item taxonomy from requisition to assign correct PIC and spend control. (9) PPH contractual agreements + SAP OA for fast transactions without PR. (10) Mobile GR with optional QR scanning. (11) External market-intelligence integration (sources TBD, must be trusted). These are the RFP scoring dimensions. Zycus positioned AI maturity, single-code-base S2P, SAP-certified adapters, workflow flexibility (ST Engineering/SM Corp/Fair Price conglomerate references), and Merlin Intake conversational AI as the fit. But fit is claimed, not proven—POC is the validation gate. The AI trust criterion is the single highest risk: if the June POC does not demonstrate governed, explainable AI with visible constraints Adonis can defend to the family owners, the AI differentiator becomes a veto and the deal is lost. Recommended move: co-design the POC use cases with Adonis around the top-3 criteria (AI governance + SME-supplier UX + demand intelligence) to prove fit where competitors are weakest, within 14 days.

bullets.full: A competitor is pushing back — Kawan Lama is running a formal four-vendor RFP shortlist: Zycus, SAP Ariba, Coupa, and GEP Worldwide, all receiving the RFP in mid-May 2026 with a 20 May response deadline and moving in parallel through clarification → POC (June) → selection (end June). GEP Worldwide is the single strongest current threat—the buyer's stated peer comparison ('quite similar to GEP', 6 May call) and the finalist Zycus must beat. Adonis asked Guillaume on 6 May 'what is the major differentiator between GEP and Zycus?', naming GEP specifically as the benchmark. Guillaume differentiated on speed to value (configurability over customization, 'on budget and on time'), superior AI roadmap ('Zycus has a clearest and more matches AI roadmap'), and the ST Engineering Singapore win case where Zycus beat GEP on SAP integration, AI maturity, and multi-BU flexibility. GEP's vulnerability: likely strong in functional S2P coverage but weaker in agentic-AI maturity and the intelligent-procurement vision (demand forecasting, market-intelligence alerts) Adonis prioritized. SAP Ariba is the second-tier threat via the incumbent ERP relationship and SAP-native integration preference, but functionally outmatched by Zycus on AI, workflow flexibility, and SME-supplier UX simplicity. Ariba's vulnerability: Zycus has certified S/4HANA adapters and a parallel-migration reference (ST Engineering), neutralizing the integration advantage, and Ariba is weaker in the 'smart procurement… intelligent insights' positioning Adonis articulated on 6 May. Coupa is an active finalist but likely third in fit—strong general P2P platform but less aligned on AI roadmap, SAP integration depth, and conglomerate multi-BU complexity vs Zycus or GEP. NextStep log reports 'Late in engagement compared with competitors', indicating at least one vendor (likely GEP or Ariba) engaged earlier, and 'under Zycus value proposition but not so mature on AI', reflecting Adonis's 6 May trust/governance concern about agentic AI ('how can we trust this AI… sometimes AI ruining something'). Zycus is well-positioned on the buyer's stated criteria (intelligent procurement, AI, SAP S/4 certified integration, conglomerate flexibility, user simplicity) but must prove AI credibility in the June POC and convert Adonis from neutral evaluator to advocate to overcome the late-entry and AI-maturity perception gaps. The wedge is intelligent procurement (Merlin demand-forecasting + market-intelligence + agentic roadmap) + speed to value + SAP parallel-migration confidence (ST Engineering blueprint for their 1 Jan 2027 timeline). The loss risk is if GEP or Ariba owns an undisclosed exec relationship or if Adonis's AI concern becomes a committee veto without a champion pushing internally for Zycus.

If the POC does not prove trustworthy AI, the AI differentiator becomes a blocker ('too risky, not ready for our culture') and the deal is lost to a 'safer' competitor (Ariba incumbent or GEP/Coupa with less agentic-AI but perceived lower-risk). bullets.full: No real champion yet — No champion identified. Adonis Sutanto is sole Zycus contact across 56 days and four calls, but he is a neutral RFP evaluator and technical project lead, not a champion—he treated all four vendors equally, pushed back on Zycus AI with trust concerns, stated explicit lowest-price expectation, and has gone silent for 41 days since last contact (21 May). A champion introduces us to power, shares competitive intel, coaches us on how to win, and pulls us forward—Adonis has done none of these. He has not introduced Economic Buyer, CFO, Supply Chain Directors, Chief Digital Officer, or family-office decision-makers. He has not shared which competitor is ahead or what GEP/Coupa/Ariba are positioning. He has not invited Zycus to any exec briefing. His engagement dropped to zero after 21 May with no response to Guillaume's 12 May reschedule offer and no outreach to schedule the technical clarification or POC he committed to. Sigit Rizqi Ramadan (Procurement Excellence, partner contact) was observer on 13 May call with no speaking role. No other buyer participant. In a four-vendor RFP where Zycus entered 'late in engagement compared with competitors' (Next Step 18 May), the vendor with a champion or coach relationship wins despite product parity. Zycus has no such relationship. Champion gap is structural and urgent.

bullets.full: Commercial conversation is live — Paper process and contracting mechanics are partially understood from the 13 May technical call but not yet formalized or scoped with legal/procurement, and no MSA, SOW, or security-review timeline has been discussed. Adonis described on 13 May a dual contractual approach: (1) PPH (legally-based contractual agreement): offline price-only contracts with vendors (no quantity or purchase commitment) for strategic-sourcing item catalogs, enabling fast transactions without a PR—'legal document'. (2) OA (Outline Agreement in SAP): SAP-integrated version of the contract for transactional execution; the eProcurement system must integrate with SAP to create the OA ('it needs to be integrated from this eProcurement, so it needs to tell the SAP to create the OA'). Both will continue in future state. This suggests contract-creation workflow and SAP-contract-integration scope must be part of the SOW, but no legal review, MSA/Order-Form negotiation, or InfoSec questionnaire has been initiated. The RFP process as described by Adonis (6 May, 12 May) goes: RFP response → technical clarification → POC → vendor selection approval → awarding, then 'deployment start June or July'. No mention of contract negotiation, legal review, security review, or procurement-channel steps between award and deployment—implying either (a) those steps are collapsed into a fast post-award phase, or (b) they are unplanned/underestimated and will cause the real slip. Given this is a $250k spend synchronized to a 1 Jan 2027 ERP migration, procurement and legal sign-off should be multi-week gates. The forcing function (Current_Contract_Expiration__c if the 9-year-old workflow platform has a contract end-date, or the 1 Jan 2027 SAP go-live) is real, but the paper-process timeline to get there is opaque. No InfoSec/compliance review mentioned despite this being a centralized procurement platform handling supplier data across subsidiaries. The 41-day silence since 21 May could include legal/procurement holds we are not aware of. Recommended move: after re-engaging Adonis on RFP status, explicitly map the post-award contract timeline (legal review, MSA/SOW, InfoSec, SAP-integration deep-dive, contract signature) and work backward from 1 Jan 2027 go-live to set a realistic close date and expose any hidden gates, within 14 days.

bullets.full: ROI / business case landed — Adonis articulated clear operational pain and business objectives across the 6 May and 13 May calls, but no quantified financial impact (cost savings, ROI, cycle-time reduction, error-rate improvement) was stated by the buyer or modeled by the relevant stakeholder 6 May Adonis described the business problem: replacing a 9-year-old workflow platform with an intelligent end-to-end S2P solution that delivers geopolitical risk insights, supply-scarcity alerts, and proactive contract management to prevent disruption ('before that kind of disruption happens, we want to know we want to have an insight whether we have to make a contract on certain products so the raw material… it will only be impacting our end products, so it will disrupt our supply chain'). He emphasized 'smart procurement' beyond governance—demand forecasting from internal requisition patterns (e.g. quarterly smartphone/notebook needs) and external cost signals (e.g. plastic price spikes from the relevant stakeholder geopolitics) to time vendor contracts optimally and avoid stockouts and price surges. On 13 May he detailed adoption-risk pain: cultural resistance, non-tech-savvy internal users, and SME suppliers at remote locations who could turn procurement into customer service if the platform is too complex. These are real, strategic pains tied to supply-chain resilience, cost control, and user adoption—but none quantified (no baseline spend under management, no cost of current manual processes, no target savings %, no ROI timeline). Guillaume acknowledged the pain and positioned Zycus AI + workflow flexibility + NTUC FairPrice SME reference as the fit, but did not build a value model. The compelling event is the 1 Jan 2027 SAP S/4HANA go-live (deployment synchronized), which is a forcing function but not a quantified business case. Without a quantified metrics case, Zycus cannot justify a price premium in a lowest-cost RFP, and the decision may default to cheapest vendor. Make building a joint ROI model (savings on manual demand analysis, scarcity-avoidance contract savings, adoption-cost reduction via simpler UX) the rank-1 move.

Guillaume positioned 'speed to value' as the counter (faster time-to-benefit offsets higher upfront cost), but no ROI model or TCO comparison has been built with Adonis yet.

Economic Buyer and C-level sponsor unmapped with 158 days to close: no CFO, Chief Digital Officer (Renaldi Tjahaja), Supply Chain Directors (Suharno Tan, Eko Sarwanto), Head of Corporate Procurement (Andreas Yonathan), or family-office decision-maker identified or engaged across 51 days of deal age and four buyer calls. Single-threaded to Adonis (Governance and Process Optimization, project lead), who is a technical evaluator, not the budget authority. On a $250k deal synchronized to a 1 Jan 2027 SAP S/4HANA migration forcing function, the EB gap is a red flag—someone controls the money and the migration priority, and we have not met them. If a competitor (GEP or Ariba) owns an exec relationship we don't see, they can win on sponsorship despite weaker product fit.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy best practices for THIS deal (refreshed by current state, no stale flags): (1) Re-engage immediately (within 7 days) to break 41-day buyer silence and confirm RFP evaluation status, technical-clarification and POC scheduling, and any competitive moves in the silent window—a cooling four-vendor RFP with 'late in engagement vs competitors' cannot afford passive waiting. (2) Map the Economic Buyer and decision-makers (CFO, Supply Chain Directors, Chief Digital Officer, or family-office principals) via an executive alignment session before the POC—frame it as 'validate the business case and SAP-synchronization plan to your 1 Jan 2027 go-live'—because a $250k deal synchronized to an ERP migration with zero exec engagement after 56 days is a structural risk; if GEP or Ariba owns an exec relationship we lack, they win on sponsorship despite weaker fit. (3) Co-design the POC use cases with Adonis around the three highest-risk proving grounds (AI governance with visible human-in-the-loop controls, self-explanatory SME-supplier UX, and demand-intelligence + scarcity-alert automation) to prove fit where competitors are weakest and address the buyer's stated veto criteria (AI trust for family-business, adoption simplicity, intelligent procurement)—the POC is the validation gate, and a generic demo will not differentiate in a four-vendor RFP; win by proving what Adonis must defend to the family owners and what GEP/Coupa/Ariba cannot match.

competitive_position.summary: Kawan Lama is running a formal four-vendor RFP shortlist: Zycus, SAP Ariba, Coupa, and GEP Worldwide, all receiving the RFP in mid-May 2026 with a 20 May response deadline and moving in parallel through clarification → POC (June) → selection (end June). GEP Worldwide is the single strongest current threat—the buyer's stated peer comparison ('quite similar to GEP', 6 May call) and the finalist Zycus must beat. Adonis asked Guillaume on 6 May 'what is the major differentiator between GEP and Zycus?', naming GEP specifically as the benchmark. Guillaume differentiated on speed to value (configurability over customization, 'on budget and on time'), superior AI roadmap ('Zycus has a clearest and more matches AI roadmap'), and the ST Engineering Singapore win case where Zycus beat GEP on SAP integration, AI maturity, and multi-BU flexibility. GEP's vulnerability: likely strong in functional S2P coverage but weaker in agentic-AI maturity and the intelligent-procurement vision (demand forecasting, market-intelligence alerts) Adonis prioritized. SAP Ariba is the second-tier threat via the incumbent ERP relationship and SAP-native integration preference, but functionally outmatched by Zycus on AI, workflow flexibility, and SME-supplier UX simplicity. Ariba's vulnerability: Zycus has certified S/4HANA adapters and a parallel-migration reference (ST Engineering), neutralizing the integration advantage, and Ariba is weaker in the 'smart procurement… intelligent insights' positioning Adonis articulated on 6 May. Coupa is an active finalist but likely third in fit—strong general P2P platform but less aligned on AI roadmap, SAP integration depth, and conglomerate multi-BU complexity vs Zycus or GEP. NextStep log reports 'Late in engagement compared with competitors', indicating at least one vendor (likely GEP or Ariba) engaged earlier, and 'under Zycus value proposition but not so mature on AI', reflecting Adonis's 6 May trust/governance concern about agentic AI ('how can we trust this AI… sometimes AI ruining something'). Zycus is well-positioned on the buyer's stated criteria (intelligent procurement, AI, SAP S/4 certified integration, conglomerate flexibility, user simplicity) but must prove AI credibility in the June POC and convert Adonis from neutral evaluator to advocate to overcome the late-entry and AI-maturity perception gaps. The wedge is intelligent procurement (Merlin demand-forecasting + market-intelligence + agentic roadmap) + speed to value + SAP parallel-migration confidence (ST Engineering blueprint for their 1 Jan 2027 timeline). The loss risk is if GEP or Ariba owns an undisclosed exec relationship or if Adonis's AI concern becomes a committee veto without a champion pushing internally for Zycus.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is Zycus's core differentiator vs. GEP/Coupa/Ariba—Guillaume led with 'agentic AI S2P power solutions', 'well recognized AI innovative leaders', 'high level of maturity engaging with AIs' (6 May)—but it is currently under-positioned and at risk of becoming a veto instead of a wedge because the buyer's AI trust/governance concern (Adonis 6 May: 'how reliable is the AI agent, how to set constraints, when we're talking about money, trust is something that need to be convinced, we know cases where agentic AI sometimes ruining something, company owned by family') has not been addressed with a demonstration. Guillaume's response was conceptual (phased adoption, beta-testing transparency, human-in-the-loop) but not tangible—no POC scheduled to prove governed, explainable AI with visible controls. The Next Step log 18 May notes 'under Zycus value proposition but not so mature on AI', indicating the buyer perceives Zycus's AI story as advanced but unproven or risky for their maturity level. Adonis explicitly asked for phased adoption (conversational/decisional AI quick wins before full agentic autonomy) which Guillaume recommended, but the POC must now demonstrate that path with visible governance (audit trails, human-approval gates, rule-constraints) on a use-case Adonis can defend to the family owners. If the POC does not prove trustworthy AI, the AI differentiator becomes a blocker ('too risky, not ready for our culture') and the deal is lost to a 'safer' competitor (Ariba incumbent or GEP/Coupa with less agentic-AI but perceived lower-risk). AI positioning is currently a claimed advantage, not a proven one, and the 41-day silence + no POC scheduled means the proof window is closing. Urgently need POC execution within 14 days to convert AI from risk to wedge.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Customer expectations are well-documented from RFP discovery (6 May) and technical clarification (13 May) calls, covering ten dimensions: (1) end-to-end S2P with workflow flexibility for multi-subsidiary/multi-industry conglomerate, (2) intelligent insights (geopolitical risk, supply scarcity, demand forecasting, market cost signals), (3) SAP ECC + S/4HANA certified integration synchronized to 1 Jan 2027 go-live, (4) user-friendly mobile-responsive interface for non-tech-savvy internal users and SME suppliers, (5) agentic AI with visible governance/constraints/audit-trails for family-business trust, (6) phased AI adoption (conversational/decisional quick wins before full autonomy), (7) centralized vendor master data (VMS integration via Kafka/Red Panda, prequalification workflow), (8) hierarchical item taxonomy, (9) PPH legal agreements + SAP OA integration, (10) external market-intelligence integration (trusted sources TBD). Zycus positioned aligned on all ten—AI maturity (Merlin agentic + Gen AI 4+ years), single-code-base S2P, SAP-certified adapters (ST Engineering parallel-migration reference), workflow flexibility (SM Corp/Fair Price conglomerate references), Merlin Intake conversational AI for UX, phased-adoption recommendation, VMS/MDM capability, and willingness to integrate external intel—but fit is claimed, not yet proven. The AI trust criterion (dimension 5) is the single highest-risk misalignment: Adonis raised it as a potential veto ('how to set constraints, family-owned company needs to be convinced on trust, we know cases where agentic AI sometimes ruining something'), and Guillaume's response (phased adoption + beta-testing transparency + human-in-the-loop) was conceptual, not demonstrated. If the POC does not show governed, explainable AI with visible controls Adonis can defend to risk-averse family owners, the AI differentiator becomes a blocker and the deal is lost. The lowest-price expectation (Adonis 6 May: 'I'll be expecting your numbers to be the lowest one') is a second misalignment: Zycus is unlikely to be cheapest among four vendors (GEP, Coupa, Ariba, Zycus), so fit depends on building a quantified ROI case showing speed-to-value + intelligent-procurement savings justify premium—this has not been done. The SME-supplier UX simplicity requirement (dimension 4) is a third proving ground—Adonis stressed it twice (6 May, 13 May: 'vendors from remote places, not tech savvy, must be self explanatory')—Guillaume referenced NTUC FairPrice SME onboarding but did not demo it. POC is the validation gate for all three. Fit summary: conceptually aligned on all ten expectations, but AI governance, ROI justification, and SME UX are unproven and at risk of exposure vs. competitors in POC.

Kawan Lama is family-owned (Adonis, 6 May call), so ultimate authority likely sits with family ownership or a C-suite proxy (Chief Digital Officer Renaldi Tjahaja, Supply Chain Directors Suharno Tan / Eko Sarwanto are account-level contacts but unconfirmed on THIS opp). value.detail: Adoption risk for non-tech-savvy internal users and SME suppliers at remote locations could veto Zycus if UX simplicity not proven in POC. Adonis stated 6 May 'adoption to change and to transform from our previous system… could be quite difficult because of our own culture… our vendors, not just big vendors… we also have small medium enterprises that they just started to have their own business… we have to make sure that our procurement platform can be engaged by those local vendors… if we're having too complex, too sophisticated application or platform that cannot be used, so it will raise more concerns, issues, questions, and then the procurement team will be becoming a customer service'. On 13 May: 'some of our vendors coming from remote places, not in suburb or not in city, so they may be not very tech savvy… please make sure that it can be self explanatory enough for the user interface'. Guillaume referenced NTUC FairPrice SME-onboarding experience 6 May but did not demonstrate. If Zycus's UX (especially for suppliers and mobile GR) is not demonstrably simpler and more intuitive than GEP/Coupa/Ariba in the POC, the buyer may choose a competitor perceived as easier to adopt, even if functionally weaker. Vulnerability is medium-high—adoption is a stated veto criterion but POC can prove it.
value.source: Avoma 6 May, 13 May calls (Adonis stressed adoption/change-mgmt pain and non-tech-savvy SME-supplier UX requirement twice)

value.detail: AI trust/governance concern is the single biggest blocker if not addressed in POC: Adonis expressed on 6 May 'how reliable is the AI agent… how is Zycus could set a constraint or set a guide rails for them to not passing some rules… when we're talking about procurement, we're talking about money… we also know there are some cases in the real world where AI agentic AI sometimes ruining something… as you may also know that is also a company owned by family, so the trust part of a system, the trust is something that need to be convinced'. NextStep log reports 'under Zycus value proposition but not so mature on AI'. If the June POC does not demonstrate governed, explainable, trustworthy AI with visible human-in-the-loop approval gates, audit trails, and rule-constraints that Adonis can see and defend to a risk-averse family-owned business, the AI positioning (our core differentiator) becomes a veto instead of a wedge, and the deal is lost to a 'safer' competitor (Ariba incumbent, or GEP/Coupa with less agentic-AI risk). Guillaume addressed this with phased-adoption guidance and beta-testing transparency, but Adonis remains cautious. This is the proving ground.

The June POC is the moment to prove governed, explainable AI: demonstrate Merlin demand-forecasting and market-intelligence agents in a live Kawan Lama use case (e.g., predict quarterly smartphone/notebook requisition volume from historical PR patterns and alert on external plastic-price spikes from a trusted data source) WITH a visible human-approval gate, audit trail, and rule-constraints that Adonis can see and trust. value.detail: Explicit lowest-price expectation in four-vendor RFP with no ROI model to justify premium. Adonis stated 6 May 'when we move forward, then we're talking about commercial, so I'll be expecting your numbers to be the lowest one, so it's the most efficient one' after Guillaume positioned on-time/on-budget delivery as differentiator. If Zycus is not cheapest among GEP, Coupa, SAP Ariba, and Zycus quotes a premium for superior AI/fit, the deal is at risk of price-driven loss unless a quantified ROI case (speed-to-value savings + intelligent-procurement contract-optimization savings + adoption-simplicity support-cost reduction) is built to justify the premium. No ROI model exists as of 1 July (56 days in). Guillaume positioned 'speed to value' conceptually but did not translate to dollars. Vulnerability is high—RFP evaluation is price-sensitive and Zycus has not armed Adonis with a business case to defend a higher price internally.
value.source: Avoma 6 May: Adonis 'I'll be expecting your numbers to be the lowest one'

value.detail: AI trust/governance concern is single biggest blocker if not addressed in POC. Adonis raised 6 May: 'how reliable is the AI agent… how is Zycus could set a constraint or set a guide rails for them to not passing some rules… when we're talking about procurement, we're talking about money… we also know there are some cases in the real world where AI agentic AI sometimes ruining something… as you may also know is also a company owned by family, so the trust part of a system, the trust is something that need to be convinced'. Next Step log 18 May: 'under Zycus value proposition but not so mature on AI'. If the POC does not demonstrate governed, explainable, trustworthy AI with visible human-in-the-loop approval gates, audit trails, and rule-constraints that Adonis can see and defend to the risk-averse family owners, the AI positioning (Zycus's core differentiator) becomes a veto instead of a wedge, and the deal is lost to a 'safer' competitor (Ariba incumbent, or GEP/Coupa with less agentic-AI risk). Guillaume addressed conceptually (phased adoption, beta-testing transparency) but did not demonstrate. Vulnerability is critical—AI differentiation is worthless if buyer perceives it as untrustworthy/risky.

business_case.evidence: Adonis articulated clear operational pain (9-year-old platform obsolete, manual demand-planning, supply-chain risk from lack of geopolitical-risk/scarcity alerts, adoption barriers for non-tech-savvy users/SME suppliers) and business objectives (smart procurement: intelligent geopolitical-risk/scarcity-signal contract-timing optimization, demand forecasting from internal requisition patterns, cost-efficiency via market-intelligence, user-friendly platform to avoid procurement-becoming-customer-service) across 6 May and 13 May calls. Compelling event is 1 Jan 2027 SAP S/4HANA go-live (forcing function: deployment must synchronize, 'we want this to be in line, same timeline'). But no quantified financial impact: no baseline spend under management, no cost of current manual processes, no target savings %, no ROI timeline, no TCO comparison. Business case is qualitative, not quantified—cannot justify a price premium in a lowest-cost RFP without numbers.
